name: Afghanistan
experiences: Kabul museum and Babur garden circuit with secure hotel-based transfers
Bamyan Valley's Buddha niches and a 4x4 day into Band-e-Amir's blue lakes
Timurid-era Herat Citadel, Friday Mosque tiles, and old-city bazaars
Mazar-i-Sharif's Blue Mosque courtyard and a shared-taxi side trip to ancient Balkh
avoid: Strong safety advisory: Afghanistan remains an active-conflict destination under Taliban governance, with ISIS-K attacks, arbitrary detention risk, weak consular support, and multiple governments advising against all travel.

Do not plan independent overland movement after dark; use hotel-arranged airport transfers, daylight-only shared taxis, or trusted 4x4 drivers, and expect checkpoints, sudden route closures, and last-minute flight changes.
Women face severe Taliban restrictions, including dress-code enforcement and possible denial of entry to parks such as Band-e-Amir; verify current local rules before every excursion.
stopoverNote: Kabul is the practical logistics base for SIMs, cash, flight changes, and driver coordination; keep a buffer night there before long road legs or any international departure.

cities.name: Kabul
cities.notes: National Museum of Afghanistan, Babur's Gardens, Darul Aman axis, Shahr-e Naw hotels, and the main Kam Air hub for domestic flights.

cities.name: Bamyan
cities.notes: Bamyan Buddha niches, Shahr-e Gholghola viewpoints, and the 4x4 route to Band-e-Amir National Park.

cities.name: Herat
cities.notes: Herat Citadel, Friday Mosque, Musalla minarets, and Persian-influenced food around the old city.

cities.name: Mazar-i-Sharif
cities.notes: Blue Mosque / Shrine of Hazrat Ali, bazaar lanes, and the classic Balkh day trip by shared taxi.

itinerary.sim: Roshan, AWCC, and Etisalat are the easiest Kabul airport/city options; budget roughly ₹120–₹240 for the SIM and ₹600–₹1,200 for a usable short-stay data top-up, with weaker coverage once you leave Kabul.

Kabul.name: Kabul
Kabul.note: Use Kabul mainly for museum time, secure compounds, and onward logistics; keep sightseeing compact and daylight-only.
experiences: Kabul museum and Babur garden circuit with secure hotel-based transfers

days.theme: Arrival logistics, Kabul Museum, and Darul Aman axis with vetted transfers

activities.name: Hotel-arranged airport transfer from Hamid Karzai International Airport
activities.tip: Do not improvise airport taxis; have the hotel or fixer confirm the driver's plate before landing.

activities.name: National Museum of Afghanistan entry
activities.tip: Recent visitor reports place admission around 100–300 AFN; carry small cash and dress conservatively.

activities.name: Darul Aman Palace exterior stop and photo break
Babur's Gardens entry
Trusted driver for museum-garden-hotel circuit

days.theme: Shahr-e Naw, Chicken Street, and a conservative buffer day before the next leg

activities.name: Chicken Street and Shahr-e Naw daylight walk with local guide
Shah-Do Shamshira Mosque exterior stop
Sakhi Shrine visit or donation
Driver-assisted cash / SIM / ticket reconfirmation loop
Early night in secure compound before the Bamyan road leg

Bamyan.name: Bamyan
Bamyan.note: Two days cover the Buddha cliff, Gholghola, and a full Band-e-Amir outing; add a third only if you want more weather buffer.
experiences: Bamyan Valley's Buddha niches and a 4x4 day into Band-e-Amir's blue lakes

days.theme: Kabul to Bamyan by shared taxi or 4x4, Buddha niches, and sunset over Shahr-e Gholghola

activities.name: Shared taxi seat or private 4x4 from Kabul to Bamyan
activities.tip: A shared seat is cheapest, but most foreign travelers use a full vehicle with a trusted driver because checkpoints and road conditions change quickly.

activities.name: Bamiyan Buddha niches and cliff complex ticket
activities.tip: Recent foreigner pricing is commonly quoted around USD 3–5 equivalent; the caves and empty niches are the draw, not reconstructed statues.

activities.name: Shahr-e Gholghola (City of Screams) access / local upkeep fee
Town taxi between hotel, Buddha cliff, and Gholghola
Dinner and tea in Bamyan center

days.theme: Band-e-Amir lakes by 4x4 with shrine viewpoints and a long daylight return

activities.name: Private 4x4 or shared local taxi to Band-e-Amir National Park
activities.tip: Expect a 2–3 hour run each way; a full-day vehicle is the most reliable format for the rough road.

activities.name: Band-e-Amir National Park / blue lakes entry fee
activities.tip: Recent foreigner pricing is usually around USD 5–10 equivalent, paid in cash at the gate or local checkpoint.

activities.name: Boat or pedal-boat hire when operating
Shrine ridge tea stop and snacks
Evening hotel-arranged reconfirmation for Kabul / Herat onward leg

Herat.name: Herat
Herat.note: One focused day is enough for the citadel, Friday Mosque, and an old-city food circuit if flights cooperate.
experiences: Timurid-era Herat Citadel, Friday Mosque tiles, and old-city bazaars

days.theme: Kam Air arrival, Herat Citadel, Friday Mosque, and Timurid-era landmarks

activities.name: Bamyan to Kabul by road, then Kam Air flight onward to Herat
activities.tip: There is no dependable Bamyan-Herat shortcut; build in a Kabul transit buffer rather than gambling on same-day recovery if the road slips.

activities.name: Herat Citadel (Qala Ikhtyaruddin) entry
activities.tip: Recent foreign visitor reports usually place entry around 300–500 AFN.

activities.name: Great Mosque of Herat / Friday Mosque donation
Musalla minarets and Gawhar Shad Mausoleum taxi loop
Old bazaar saffron, carpet, and tea stop

Mazar-i-Sharif.name: Mazar-i-Sharif
Mazar-i-Sharif.note: Two days cover the Blue Mosque plus the easy Balkh excursion; use flights instead of long overland hops whenever possible.
experiences: Mazar-i-Sharif's Blue Mosque courtyard and a shared-taxi side trip to ancient Balkh

days.theme: Flight into Mazar, Blue Mosque courtyard, and bazaar lanes around the shrine

activities.name: Kam Air domestic connection from Herat via Kabul into Mazar-i-Sharif
activities.tip: Domestic schedules change fast; reconfirm the night before and again on the day of travel.

activities.name: Blue Mosque / Shrine of Hazrat Ali entry
activities.tip: There is normally no admission fee; carry a small note only if you want to make a donation and dress conservatively.

activities.name: Rawza courtyard pigeon-feeding and tilework circuit
City taxi hops between airport, hotel, and shrine district
Evening qabili palau and kebab dinner

days.theme: Shared taxi to ancient Balkh with Green Mosque and No Gombad ruins

activities.name: Shared taxi or private cab to Balkh and back
Green Mosque of Balkh donation
No Gombad / Haji Piyada Mosque site visit
Balkh bazaar tea and bread stop
Return to Mazar for airport buffer or extra shrine time

connections.method: Shared taxi via the Bamyan highway or a hotel-arranged 4x4; leave at dawn, keep it daylight-only, and avoid assuming the road will stay open in bad weather.
Return by 4x4/shared taxi to Kabul first, then use a Kam Air or Ariana domestic flight onward to Herat; this is slow but far safer than forcing an improvised westbound road run.
Domestic flight via Kabul is the practical option; overland only makes sense for essential travel with current local security advice and much more time.

extras: April to June and September to October are the workable windows: mountain roads around Bamyan are clearer, Kabul is less icy, and Herat/Mazar avoid the worst midsummer heat.

Afghanistan is not a casual backpacking add-on in 2026. Re-check live travel advisories before booking and again before every intercity leg because Taliban rules, checkpoint conditions, and flight operations can change without notice.

Kam Air is the most useful domestic carrier for Kabul-Herat and Kabul-Mazar sectors, but keep printed or screenshot copies of tickets and leave at least one buffer night before any international departure.
Cash is king: ATMs can fail, cards are unreliable, and foreign travelers usually need a mix of AFN for daily use plus emergency USD kept separately.
Women travelers should plan conservatively with local guidance, full-coverage clothing, and the expectation that some leisure sites or parks may deny entry under current Taliban policies.
